title: The Death of the CRT Monitor
author: Cade Metz, PC Magazine
abstract: PC Magazine / PCMag.com article (Nov 1 2002, Cade Metz) on the LCD-vs-CRT monitor transition. Peter Kastner, Aberdeen Group vice president and senior researcher, provides key market data: LCDs at 40% of desktop-display market with ~2M units/month at $800M revenue/month (average $400/unit). iSuppli/Stanford Resources' Rhonda Alexander offers more conservative 29% LCD share, with tracking from 3% (Q1 2000) → 5% (early 2001) → 21% (Jan 2002). Both analysts predict LCDs will outnumber CRTs as the standard PC monitor in 2003. Planar Systems' Rob Baumgartner addresses video-latency concerns. U Minn Carlson School professors Murtha and Lenway predict LCDs will outnumber CRTs 5:1 by 2006. Kastner's pre-CRO Aberdeen era (VP/senior researcher).
importance_rationale: Documents the moment (late 2002) when LCD-CRT transition crossed the analyst-consensus tipping point. Kastner and Alexander both publicly predicting LCD primacy in 2003 gave OEMs cover to accelerate LCD standardization.
relevance_rationale: CRT fully phased out from mainstream PC monitors by 2010; LCD/LED now default. Historical value for display-industry transitions; less relevant to current OLED/mini-LED transitions.
prescience_rationale: Kastner and Alexander's 2003 LCD-dominance prediction was validated — 2003 was the first year LCD shipments exceeded CRT shipments in monitors per DisplaySearch/iSuppli data. By 2006 LCDs were essentially universal in new PCs; by 2010 CRT was a niche/legacy category only. Murtha/Lenway's 5:1 by 2006 prediction was also roughly correct.
